range of inverse equals domain of original

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A graph consists of the points \((0, 2), (1, 4), (2, 6), (3, 8)\). Does this graph represent a function, and why?

options.A: Yes, because each \(x\)-value has exactly one \(y\)-value.
options.B: Yes, because it passes through the origin.
options.C: No, because it is a straight line.
options.D: No, because the \(y\)-values are all positive.

questions.hint: Check if any \(x\)-value appears with two different \(y\)-values.
questions.explanation: Each input \(x\) (0,1,2,3) appears exactly once, each paired with a unique \(y\). Thus the vertical line test is passed, and the graph represents a function. Option A correctly states the reason.

explanations_tiered.definition: A graph represents a function if and only if no vertical line intersects the graph at more than one point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: All \(x\)-values (0,1,2,3) are distinct, so each input maps to exactly one output. This passes the vertical line test.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each input (0,1,2,3) goes to only one output, so it's a function.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: No \(x\)-value is repeated with different \(y\)-values. Hence the relation is a function.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The set of points is a discrete subset of the line \(y = 2x + 2\), which is a function because the projection onto the x-axis is injective for these points.
reasoning_steps: Check if any x appears more than once → no.
Thus each x has a unique y → function.
misconceptions: Assuming all straight lines are functions (they are, but that's not the reason here; vertical lines are not functions).
Thinking positivity of y or passing through origin matters for being a function.

glossary_entry.definition: A relation in which each input (domain element) is paired with exactly one output (range element).

flashcard.front: How do you know if a set of points represents a function?
flashcard.back: Check that no x-value appears more than once with different y-values.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does the set \((0,2),(1,4),(2,6),(3,8)\) represent a function?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Yes, because each x-value appears only once, so each input has one output.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A relation in which each input (domain element) is paired with exactly one output (range element).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that the vertical line test is a graphical version of the unique-output condition.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply the vertical line test to identify functions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. All x-values are unique, so it passes the vertical line test. Options B, C, D are incorrect reasons; being a straight line does not disqualify a function (except vertical line).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the domain of \(f(x) = x^{-\frac{1}{3}}\)?
questions.hint: \(x^{-1/3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{x}}\). Cube roots are defined for all real numbers, but denominator cannot be zero.
questions.explanation: \(f(x) = x^{-1/3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{x}}\). The cube root is defined for all real \(x\), including negatives. The only restriction is that the denominator cannot be zero, so \(x \neq 0\). Hence domain = \(\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}\). Option C is correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: For \(f(x) = x^{-1/n}\) with odd \(n\), the domain is all nonzero real numbers because odd roots are defined for negatives but division by zero is disallowed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cube root is defined for all reals, so only restriction is \(x \neq 0\) from the reciprocal.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You can take cube root of any number, but you can't divide by zero. So \(x\) can be anything except 0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(x^{-1/3} = 1/\sqrt[3]{x}\). \(\sqrt[3]{x}\) is defined for all \(x\), but \(x=0\) makes denominator zero. Hence domain = \(\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The function is defined for all nonzero reals and is odd: \(f(-x) = -f(x)\). It has a vertical asymptote at \(x=0\) and horizontal asymptote at \(y=0\).
reasoning_steps: Cube root defined for all real x.
Thus domain = all real numbers except 0.
misconceptions: Excluding negative numbers because of even root confusion (but cube root is odd root).
Including 0 in domain (division by zero).

glossary_entry.term: Power function with negative odd-denominator exponent
glossary_entry.definition: A function of the form \(f(x) = x^{-1/n}\) with \(n\) odd; domain and range are all nonzero reals.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Yes, because cube root of -8 is -2, so \((-8)^{-1/3} = -1/2\). Only \(x=0\) is excluded.
publishing_metadata.glossary_term: Power function with negative odd-denominator exponent
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function of the form \(f(x) = x^{-1/n}\) with \(n\) odd; domain and range are all nonzero reals.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine domain of power functions with negative rational exponents
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. \(x^{-1/3} = 1/\sqrt[3]{x}\). Cube root defined for all reals, but denominator zero when x=0, so domain = all reals except 0. Option A includes 0; Option B excludes negatives; Option D only negatives.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the range of \(f(x) = x^{-\frac{2}{3}}\)?
questions.hint: \(x^{-2/3} = \frac{1}{(\sqrt[3]{x})^2}\). The denominator is always positive (except at 0), so the output is always positive.
questions.explanation: \(f(x) = x^{-2/3} = \frac{1}{(\sqrt[3]{x})^2}\). The cube root squared is always nonnegative, and zero only when \(x=0\), which is excluded. Thus the denominator is always positive, so \(f(x) > 0\) for all \(x \neq 0\). As \(x \to \pm\infty\), \(f(x) \to 0\); as \(x \to 0\), \(f(x) \to \infty\). Hence range = \((0, \infty)\).

explanations_tiered.definition: The range of \(f(x) = x^{-2/3}\) is all positive real numbers because the function is even and positive for all nonzero \(x\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rewrite as \(1/(x^{2/3}) = 1/((\sqrt[3]{x})^2)\). Since the denominator is always positive (square of a real number), the output is always positive. It attains every positive value.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Squaring makes everything positive, so the denominator is always positive. The function value is always positive, and it can be any positive number.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(x^{-2/3}\) is even: \((-x)^{-2/3} = x^{-2/3}\). For \(x>0\), as \(x\) varies from 0+ to ∞, \(x^{-2/3}\) varies from ∞ down to 0. Hence range = \((0,\infty)\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The function is continuous on \((0,\infty)\) and even, so its range on \(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}\) is the same as on \((0,\infty)\). Since \(\lim_{x\to0^+} x^{-2/3} = \infty\) and \(\lim_{x\to\infty} x^{-2/3} = 0\), by the intermediate value theorem it attains all positive values.
reasoning_steps: Note that \(x^{2/3} = (\sqrt[3]{x})^2 \ge 0\), zero only at x=0.
As x varies, f(x) takes all positive values.
misconceptions: Assuming range includes negative numbers (but even numerator exponent makes it even function, output positive).
Including 0 in range (never zero because numerator is 1).

glossary_entry.term: Even power function with negative exponent
glossary_entry.definition: A function of the form \(f(x) = x^{-m/n}\) with \(m\) even; the range is \((0,\infty)\) and the domain is all nonzero reals.

flashcard.back: \((0,\infty)\); it is always positive and never zero.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Because \(x^{-2/3} = 1/((\sqrt[3]{x})^2)\), and the square of any real number is nonnegative and zero only at x=0, which is excluded.
publishing_metadata.glossary_term: Even power function with negative exponent
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function of the form \(f(x) = x^{-m/n}\) with \(m\) even; the range is \((0,\infty)\) and the domain is all nonzero reals.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Graph it: two branches in quadrants I and II, both above x-axis.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine range of power functions with negative rational exponents
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(x^{-2/3} = 1/((\sqrt[3]{x})^2)\) is always positive, tends to 0 as |x|→∞, and tends to ∞ as x→0, so range = \((0,\infty)\). Option A incorrectly includes negatives; Option C includes 0; Option D includes negatives.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The graph of a function \(f\) passes through the point \((2, 5)\). Through which point must the graph of \(f^{-1}\) pass?
questions.hint: If \((a, b)\) is on the graph of \(f\), then \((b, a)\) is on the graph of \(f^{-1}\).
questions.explanation: For a function and its inverse, ordered pairs are swapped. Since \(f(2) = 5\), then \(f^{-1}(5) = 2\), so \((5, 2)\) lies on the graph of \(f^{-1}\). Option B is correct. Option A is the original point; Option C is a reflection across the origin; Option D suggests reciprocal coordinates.

source_trace.content_anchor: graph of inverse is reflection across y=x

explanations_tiered.definition: The graph of \(f^{-1}\) is the reflection of the graph of \(f\) across the line \(y = x\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Inverse swaps inputs and outputs. Input 2 gives output 5, so input 5 gives output 2.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since \(f(2) = 5\), then \(f^{-1}(5) = 2\). Therefore the point \((5,2)\) lies on the graph of \(f^{-1}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The graph of the inverse is obtained by reflecting across \(y=x\). The reflection of \((2,5)\) is \((5,2)\).
reasoning_steps: Recall: if (a,b) is on f, then (b,a) is on f^{-1}.
misconceptions: Keeping the point the same (confusing function with its inverse).
Reflecting across origin or taking reciprocals.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: If (a,b) on f, then (b,a) on f^{-1}.

glossary_entry.definition: The set of points \((x,y)\) such that \((y,x)\) is on the graph of the original function; the reflection of the original graph across the line \(y = x\).

flashcard.front: If (2,5) is on the graph of f, what point is on f^{-1}?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How is the graph of an inverse function related to the original function's graph?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It is the reflection across the line y = x.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The set of points \((x,y)\) such that \((y,x)\) is on the graph of the original function; the reflection of the original graph across the line \(y = x\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use tracing paper to physically reflect across y=x.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Relate the graph of a function to the graph of its inverse
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. If (2,5) is on f, then (5,2) is on f^{-1} because coordinates swap. Option A is the original point; Option C would be a 180° rotation; Option D is incorrect.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A student claims that \(f(x) = x^2\) with domain \(\mathbb{R}\) has an inverse function because \(f(2) = 4\) and \(f(-2) = 4\). Why is this claim incorrect?

options.B: The function is not defined at zero.
options.D: The range is not all real numbers.

questions.hint: A function must be one-to-one (injective) to have an inverse function.
questions.explanation: The student's own example shows \(f(2) = f(-2) = 4\), so two different inputs give the same output. Thus \(f\) is not one-to-one, so it does not have an inverse function (unless the domain is restricted). Option A correctly identifies the problem.

source_trace.content_anchor: function must be one-to-one to have inverse

explanations_tiered.definition: A function has an inverse function if and only if it is one-to-one (injective), meaning distinct inputs produce distinct outputs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The example \(f(2)=f(-2)=4\) shows that \(f\) is not one-to-one, so it fails the horizontal line test and does not have an inverse function on domain \(\mathbb{R}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two different x's (2 and -2) give the same y (4). That means you can't reverse the process uniquely — inverse would have to send 4 to two different numbers.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For an inverse function to exist, \(f(a) = f(b)\) must imply \(a = b\). Here \(2 \neq -2\) but \(f(2) = f(-2)\), violating injectivity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The function \(f(x) = x^2\) on \(\mathbb{R}\) is not injective, hence not bijective onto its range. It can be made invertible by restricting the domain to \([0,\infty)\) or \((-\infty,0]\).
reasoning_steps: Recall: inverse function exists iff function is one-to-one.
Thus f is not one-to-one → no inverse function.
misconceptions: Thinking every function has an inverse (only one-to-one functions do).
Believing that the existence of f(a)=b guarantees an inverse (fails if another input also maps to b).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: One-to-one: a≠b ⇒ f(a)≠f(b). f(x)=x^2 on R is not one-to-one.

glossary_entry.definition: A function where each output is produced by exactly one input; equivalently, f(a)=f(b) implies a=b.

flashcard.front: Why doesn't \(f(x)=x^2\) on \(\mathbb{R}\) have an inverse function?
flashcard.back: It is not one-to-one: f(2)=f(-2)=4, so two inputs give the same output.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What condition must a function satisfy to have an inverse function?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It must be one-to-one (injective): different inputs must give different outputs.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function where each output is produced by exactly one input; equivalently, f(a)=f(b) implies a=b.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: f(x)=x^2 on R: not one-to-one → no inverse function.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use horizontal line test: y=4 hits parabola twice at x=2 and x=-2.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine whether a function is invertible
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The function is not one-to-one (f(2)=f(-2)=4). Options B, C, D are false: f is defined at 0, continuous, and its range is [0,∞), which is fine for invertibility if domain is restricted, but not the issue here.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A taxi company charges a flat fee of Birr 50 plus Birr 12 per kilometer. Which function represents the total cost \(C\) in Birr for a trip of \(x\) kilometers?
questions.hint: Total cost = (cost per km × number of km) + flat fee.
questions.explanation: The cost per kilometer is 12 Birr, so the variable cost is \(12x\). The flat fee is 50 Birr, added regardless of distance. Thus \(C(x) = 12x + 50\). Option B is correct; Option A swaps coefficients; Option C combines incorrectly; Option D ignores \(x\).

explanations_tiered.definition: A linear cost function has the form \(C(x) = mx + b\), where \(m\) is the marginal cost per unit and \(b\) is the fixed cost.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Variable cost = 12 Birr per km × x km = 12x. Fixed cost = 50 Birr. Total = 12x + 50.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each kilometer costs 12 Birr, so x kilometers cost 12x. Then add the flat fee 50.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is a linear function with slope 12 (marginal cost) and y-intercept 50 (fixed cost).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify variable cost per unit: 12 Birr/km.
Step 2: Variable cost for x km = 12x.
misconceptions: Adding flat fee to each kilometer (50x + 12).
Multiplying flat fee by x.
Forgetting the variable altogether.

glossary_entry.definition: A function of the form \(C(x) = mx + b\) where \(m\) is the cost per unit and \(b\) is the fixed cost.

flashcard.front: What is the cost function for a Birr 50 flat fee plus Birr 12 per km?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you write a cost function with a flat fee of 50 and per-unit cost of 12?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: \(C(x) = 12x + 50\), where x is the number of units (kilometers).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function of the form \(C(x) = mx + b\) where \(m\) is the cost per unit and \(b\) is the fixed cost.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to slope-intercept form y = mx + b.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Model real-world situations with linear functions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Total cost = 12x + 50. Option A has coefficients swapped; Option C adds incorrectly; Option D omits x.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A company's profit function is \(P(x) = 8x - 1200\), where \(x\) is the number of units sold. How many units must be sold to break even (\(P(x) = 0\))?
questions.explanation: Break-even occurs when \(P(x) = 0\). So \(8x - 1200 = 0 \Rightarrow 8x = 1200 \Rightarrow x = 150\). Option B is correct. Option A gives \(8(100)-1200 = -400\); Option C gives \(8(200)-1200 = 400\); Option D gives \(8(50)-1200 = -800\).

explanations_tiered.definition: The break-even point is where profit equals zero, meaning total revenue equals total cost.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Break-even means no profit, no loss. Solve 8x - 1200 = 0: add 1200, divide by 8 gives x = 150.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(8x - 1200 = 0 \Rightarrow 8x = 1200 \Rightarrow x = 150\). Selling 150 units covers all costs.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The break-even quantity is found by setting the linear profit function to zero: \(x_{BE} = \frac{\text{fixed cost}}{\text{price per unit} - \text{variable cost per unit}}\). Here, \(x_{BE} = 1200/8 = 150\).
reasoning_steps: Set profit function equal to zero.
Add constant term to both sides.
misconceptions: Solving 8x = 1200 incorrectly (e.g., 1200/8 = 100 or 200).
Forgetting to set P(x)=0 and solving 8x - 1200 = something else.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Break-even: P(x)=0 → x = fixed cost / (price - variable cost).

glossary_entry.definition: The quantity at which total revenue equals total cost, resulting in zero profit.

flashcard.front: How many units must be sold to break even if P(x)=8x-1200?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the break-even quantity for P(x)=8x-1200?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The quantity at which total revenue equals total cost, resulting in zero profit.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Check: P(150)=0; P(149) negative; P(151) positive.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve real-world problems using linear functions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Solve 8x - 1200 = 0 → x = 150. Option A (100) gives P= -400; Option C (200) gives P=400; Option D (50) gives P= -800.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The cost to produce \(x\) items is \(C(x) = 500 + 20x\) and each item sells for Birr 40. What is the revenue function \(R(x)\)?
questions.hint: Revenue = (price per item) × (number of items sold).
questions.explanation: Revenue is the income from sales, calculated as price per unit times quantity sold. Here price = 40 Birr, quantity = x, so \(R(x) = 40x\). Option A is correct. Option B incorrectly adds fixed cost; Option C uses cost per unit; Option D gives profit.

explanations_tiered.definition: Revenue is the total income from sales, calculated as selling price per unit times the number of units sold.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each item sells for Birr 40, so selling x items yields \(40x\) Birr. Fixed costs do not affect revenue.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Revenue is just price times quantity: 40 × x = 40x.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(R(x) = 40x\). This does not include costs; profit would be \(R(x) - C(x) = 40x - (500+20x) = 20x - 500\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Revenue is a linear function through the origin with slope equal to the selling price. It is independent of cost structure.
reasoning_steps: Identify selling price per unit: 40 Birr.
misconceptions: Adding fixed cost to revenue (fixed cost belongs to cost, not revenue).
Confusing revenue with profit (revenue minus cost).

glossary_entry.definition: A function \(R(x)\) that gives the total income from selling \(x\) units at a given price per unit.

flashcard.front: What is the revenue function if each item sells for Birr 40?
flashcard.back: \(R(x) = 40x\), where x is the number of items sold.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you calculate revenue given price per unit?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Multiply price per unit by the number of units sold: R(x) = price × x.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A function \(R(x)\) that gives the total income from selling \(x\) units at a given price per unit.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Distinguish between revenue, cost, and profit clearly.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Model business scenarios with linear functions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Revenue = price × quantity = 40x. Option B incorrectly adds fixed cost; Option C uses cost per unit; Option D is profit (revenue minus cost).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A firm's cost function is \(C(x) = 500 + 20x\) and revenue function is \(R(x) = 40x\). What is the profit when 30 units are produced and sold?
questions.hint: Profit = Revenue − Cost. Compute \(P(30) = R(30) - C(30)\).
questions.explanation: \(P(x) = R(x) - C(x) = 40x - (500 + 20x) = 20x - 500\). At \(x = 30\): \(P(30) = 20(30) - 500 = 600 - 500 = 100\) Birr. Option A is correct. Option B would be for \(x=35\); Option C is \(R(30)\); Option D is \(C(30)\) maybe?

explanations_tiered.definition: Profit is the difference between revenue and cost: \(P(x) = R(x) - C(x)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: First compute revenue at x=30: \(40 \times 30 = 1200\). Cost: \(500 + 20 \times 30 = 1100\). Profit = 1200 - 1100 = 100.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Money in (revenue) minus money out (cost). At 30 units: revenue 1200, cost 1100, so profit 100.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The profit function simplifies to \(P(x) = 20x - 500\). At \(x=30\), \(P=600-500=100\).
misconceptions: Using revenue only as profit (ignoring cost).
Miscomputing cost (e.g., 500+20×30 = 500+600 = 1100 is correct; adding incorrectly gives 1500).

glossary_entry.definition: The function \(P(x) = R(x) - C(x)\) representing the profit from producing and selling x units.

flashcard.front: What is the profit when selling 30 units if R(x)=40x and C(x)=500+20x?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you compute profit given revenue and cost functions?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Profit = Revenue − Cost; evaluate both at the given quantity and subtract.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The function \(P(x) = R(x) - C(x)\) representing the profit from producing and selling x units.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ensure students compute both R and C before subtracting.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. R(30)=1200, C(30)=1100, profit = 100. Option B (200) might come from 20×30-400; Option C (600) is R(30); Option D (700) might be C(30) misinterpreted.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A company has fixed costs of Birr 2000 and variable costs of Birr 5 per unit. If the selling price is Birr 15 per unit, what is the effect on the break-even point if fixed costs increase by Birr 500?
questions.hint: Break-even quantity = fixed costs / (price − variable cost).
questions.explanation: Original break-even: \(x = \frac{2000}{15-5} = \frac{2000}{10} = 200\) units. New fixed costs = 2500, so new break-even = \(\frac{2500}{10} = 250\) units. Increase of 50 units. Option B is correct. Other options miscalculate the change.

explanations_tiered.definition: Break-even quantity = fixed costs ÷ (selling price per unit − variable cost per unit).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Original BE = 2000/10 = 200. New BE = 2500/10 = 250. Increase of 50 units.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Higher fixed costs mean you need to sell more to cover costs. Here extra 500 Birr fixed cost needs 50 more units at 10 Birr contribution each.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Contribution margin per unit = 15 - 5 = 10. BE = FC / 10. FC ↑ by 500 → BE ↑ by 500/10 = 50 units.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The break-even quantity is directly proportional to fixed costs when contribution margin is constant. ΔBE = ΔFC / (price − VC) = 500/10 = 50.
worked_solution: Step 1: Contribution margin per unit = 15 - 5 = 10 Birr.
reasoning_steps: Calculate new break-even quantity after cost increase.
misconceptions: Dividing fixed cost increase by price alone (500/15 ≈ 33).
Dividing by variable cost alone (500/5=100).
Assuming break-even decreases when fixed costs increase.

glossary_entry.definition: The number of units that must be sold for total revenue to equal total cost.

flashcard.front: If fixed costs increase by 500 Birr and contribution margin is 10 Birr, how does break-even change?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does an increase in fixed costs affect break-even quantity?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Break-even quantity increases by (increase in fixed cost) / (contribution margin per unit).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The number of units that must be sold for total revenue to equal total cost.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use the formula x_BE = FC / (P − VC) to analyze sensitivity.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Analyze the effect of changes in costs on break-even point
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Original BE = 2000/(15-5)=200; new BE = 2500/10=250; increase of 50 units. Option A (decrease) is wrong direction; Options C and D miscalculate the change.
